speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 26, 1995. This is an interview with Lillian Eckstein. I am Amy Friedman, the interviewer. We are in New York in the United States of America. The interview is in English, and we are on tape 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Can ?] [? it ?] see [? us? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were talking about the Oberschlesien and the work you did. Can you tell us a typical day when you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got up early in the morning, and we had to stay [? at ?] appell to count how many are alive. And then we were marched to work. They gave us shovels, and we were digging, digging so that, again, the tanks should fall in there. I never knew. I was wondering all this time whether the tanks really were-- fell-- whether they fell in there. But I never got around to go to that area yet. I will, I hope. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, we worked the day through, and we came back. They gave us hot soup and a slice of bread. And we were ordered to go into our bunk, into our bed, and that's where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were with your sisters in Auschwitz. Were you still with your sisters at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were together and in Oberschlesien. When we-- they took us away from Oberschlesien because the Russians were approaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stay in Oberschlesien, if we can.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how you and your sisters supported each other during that time? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as sisters would, with loving feelings. We cling together to keep warm most of all. And we were talking about food. That was our subject, not the current events, because we did not have any way of-- any newspapers or any radios. So we would talk about the food that we used to prepare for Sabbath. And emotionally, we were satisfied. And we were joking. We were trying to keep-- we were trying to keep [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] normal because we did not want to crack. A lot of them did crack emotionally. They had severe nervous breakdowns. And I vowed that I will not get to that point no matter what. I saw-- I saw what happened to those girls when they got to that point. They were shot in their head. Of course, they started screaming and running out, and they were shot to death. So this is why we tried to-- to keep quiet. And that's how we survived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did other people survive through that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were mothers and sisters and brothers-- mothers and-- mothers and daughters. And I witnessed some horrible situations, where the daughters were stealing the-- the bread from their mothers. They-- we-- we got that bread, and we would just take a bite and put it away for later. So when we were starving, so we can have something in our stomachs. And when the mothers were already sleeping, their daughters took the bread. And they would-- I prefer not to talk about this situation. We-- we-- the three of us, we stuck together to keep warm, to keep sane, and that's how we managed to get through that period.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? When ?] you were still in Oberschlesien, how about the Germans? What do you remem-- do you remember anyone who was particularly cruel or kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. There were some. And I can honestly tell you they felt terribly guilty. They tried to-- they were afraid to talk to us. They realized that the war is approaching-- the-- the war is coming to an end, and they didn't know what to do. One of them, particularly, was extremely vicious. He was a very tall, extremely awkward, ugly-looking man. He used to go out and beating people for no reason. He had a wooden stake hanging down from the side of his pants. And when he hit you-- you-- we had no-- we only were bones with skin, and it really used to break bones with that. And we-- they couldn't lift those shovels with-- with the earth. And-- and when he realized that we were falling apart, he would just yell out, [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, go out, go on. And there were some young ones that they looked with such guilt, with such pain, in their eyes. But they were afraid to protect us in any way, because they were afraid of themselves. And that was the life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the man's name with the stick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- no, no. He would just yell out that-- there was [NON-ENGLISH], the Sonderkommando-- I mean, that you're going to wind up in the gas chamber, he used to yell out, you know. He-- in my opinion, he must have been intoxicated, because a normal person doesn't behave like a dog like that. I mean, it-- it was inhuman what he used to do. When the people fell, they couldn't do work anymore, he would kick him. And he had boots with-- on the back it had like a piece of iron. He would kick them with that. A sadist like this, in my opinion, should-- should wind up in jail for the rest of his life, himself. And who knows how many of them are still alive. And after 50 years, I just hope that something like this can never happen again. Because if you-- if you are guilty, you're being punished, you know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we move away from Oberschlesien, I'd like to go back to Auschwitz, if we could. And if you could talk about your sisters and you in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister was married, and she had two children. She was married to a schoolteacher, and her husband was taken to Poland to work as a-- as a medic in a hospital. And when we heard that they set a fire to that-- to that hospital because they-- they contracted typhoid. And they were afraid that it's going to spread, because those days they didn't have antibiotics. And so they decided just to burn down the whole hospital, and they did. And he perished there. Her two children, when we arrived to Auschwitz and my mother took them. And they went with my mother to the gas chamber, the two children. And my sister came with us. For a long time, she didn't talk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She-- she didn't talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was afraid that she's going to do something crazy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But she didn't. And we went to Oberschlesien together. And may I tell you the story what happened to her? Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: One night the Russians were approaching, and they told us to get out. And they took us, and they put us on a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on buses-- not buses. Where they deliver cattles, [? one ?] of those-- those-- trucks. And they drove us deeper into Germany. And we got off the-- those trucks, and they took us into a barn. And that was already in Germany, in Brandenburg. And we went into that barn, and they told us to go and sleep. There was a lot of hay, which was prepared for the cows. And my sisters were next to me. And my older sister, Ibi, came and kissed me and said something in my ears, that she no longer can take this. And so I was so tired and so-- so broken I didn't realize what she was up to. And she escaped-- she didn't escape. She went into another barn, and the owner's son, or whoever he was, saved her and eventually married her. In the morning, when we got out from them they counted us again, and she was missing. So the person, the-- the Gestapo who counted us, they wanted to know where my sister was. And I-- we didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me, and they beat me severely, so much that I couldn't walk after that. I should tell them where she was, and I really did not know where she was. [SIGHS] So they took us to march out from there. I don't know how, because after that beating I was in pretty bad shape. How did you recover? How-- how did you get taken care of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, my other sister, and my friends, they picked me up under my arms so I-- because I couldn't walk. So they were just dragging me so they shouldn't leave me behind, because they were going to shoot me there. So we wound up on the train, and they took us to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to when you didn't know where your sister was, what did you think when she wasn't in the line? What was going through your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something in-- in my subconscious mind. I-- I remembered when she kissed me and said that she no longer can take this anymore. I figured that she committed suicide. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't-- I didn't know what to think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I told you, we were just like zombies. And whatever happened, we-- no matter what happened to us, we were just ready for anything after this. We didn't care really what happens anymore. Just-- my main goal was just to die, because I wanted out of this misery. And somehow it didn't work out. I didn't have the guts to kill myself. After your beating, when you were so broken, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aside from the help that your sister gave, how did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe God can answer you that. I'm here. I'm here in a free country. I've raised two beautiful children. The-- [SIGHS]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want-- after the beating, go back to that-- did you want to die? Did you want to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't want to live. I didn't want to live because I realized that-- that my mind is going, that I'm cracking emotionally. And after that, what good-- what good is it? I didn't see a way out. I was sure that my sister committed suicide. I was sure of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you find out that she was living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out-- I used-- when I came to this country, I was lighting yahrzeit candles. You know what that is? After a dead person, every year you light candles. I used to light a candle. Years later, the Red Cross, through the Red Cross, I found out she-- she went to Red Cross in Warsaw. And I was acquiring-- inquiring about my family. That's how I found her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you go back to her experience that you now have heard about? Bring us to the hiding and the finding. Can you tell us that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I didn't-- I don't-- I didn't know any details. When I finally brought her to this country-- she came by boat. And I went on the boat with the permission to-- to look for her, because I was very anxious for weeks to see her. And I passed her by several times, and I did not recognize her. She didn't recognize me neither. I passed by. And so she got very scared that nobody's picking her up with her family. And my brother-in-law was there, too, the one who saved her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you the story of how it all happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, later on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: --she told me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: --what happened, that she-- she escaped. And the owner's son apparently was hiding her. And-- and when the war was over, he married her, and they had three daughters. And she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it risky for him--

speaker: interviewee
text: I [? beg-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: --to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [? beg-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a risk for him to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, absolutely. I've told you before, there's some very good people-- Gentiles or Jews or whatever. There are some, especially young people. I realize, even in this country, young people are easier and eager to help. People who had the very hard life, they are more careful, looking out for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're back in Oberschlesien, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and you've been beaten. Tell us what happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they put a lot of pressure on me. And no matter what I said or did, they did not believe me that I don't know. And I really didn't know. So I told them, you can kill me, and I wish you would. I still don't know where she is. And they were going in the hay. You know, they have one of these things that you stick-- it's like a fork. They were going there because they were under the impression that she was hiding underneath the hay, which some of them did. And I don't-- I hate to tell you what happened to those people. They were going down and trying to see if she is underneath the hay. And I-- no matter what I did, no matter how I pleaded with them, begging to shoot me, just I don't know. I just simply don't know. And I didn't know. And so they-- they just [INAUDIBLE] ugly guy from Oberschlesien who did this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people hiding in the hay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They put the bullets-- when they brought them out, they put a bullet straight into their head and just let them die there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't blame them from trying to-- trying to hide, because we didn't know where they're taking us. As a matter of fact, where we wound up after Oberschlesien and Bergen-Belsen, that's where the real problems started. Because they were prepared in Auschwitz and in Birkenau. They had the facilities all laid out, all planned out. Bergen-Belsen was not prepared for such-- so many people. So when they brought a lot of people from all over to Bergen-Belsen, they contracted typhoid, including myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from Oberschlesien to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. By trucks, by walking, by trains. I don't know how long the trip took to get there. When we got there, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about each of those things? The truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trucks-- certain places they had to stop, because the English were approaching from one side. The Americans were bombing, coming. And the Russians were coming. And they were like taking people from one side to another so they can save themselves. And some of the Gestapo, while we were on the way they were changing clothes into civilian clothes. That's when we realized that there's something going on. And that's when we decided, perhaps, let's try to stay alive so the world would know what-- what happen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so we-- that's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you decided that you wanted to live, how did you change your behavior? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I prepared myself emotionally, that if I am-- if I remain alive, I want to tell the world what a so-called-- in the 20th century-- such barbaric, inhuman, cruel people, doctors, can do to people. So-- so we just decided. But when my real breaking point was when I arrived to Bergen-Belsen, and I contracted typhoid. I lost my hair. I didn't-- there was at one point where we wanted to know-- I didn't remember anything-- where, who I really was. Because at the time, the temperature was-- must have been so high that my lips were just peeling. The skin was coming off. And my-- the bruises from-- from the beating apparently, and the bottom of my feet were completely-- the skin was just coming off. And there were mountains and mountains of dead people. So everybody was walking around, or some of them were laying down. And you walked a few steps, and you laid down again. And the smell-- the-- the temperature from this disease was so bad that [SIGHS] it's-- it's some kind of a miracle that someone can survive something like that, laying there between dead people for days, not knowing where or what-- what is going on, and without food and without water. And look at me now. I'm here. And if anybody is ever going to try to tell that this never happened, [SOBBING] it did happen. It did happen. It happened to me. [SOBBING] Please believe me. No professor can ever deny this, that it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think that the German who are responsible for this [CRYING] should pay a price with their families. I don't wish anything bad to innocent people. But why? Why did they do this? [SOBBING] Why? We are not criminals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am sorry to tell you that my uncle was a composer in Germany. My mother was working in the postmaster, in the post office in the first World War. They were not ignorant people. Why did they do this? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I hope that these professors who are trying to brainwash people that it never happened, yes, it did happen. And don't try to deny it. Don't try it, because it's going to backfire on you. I'm sorry. You're doing wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: It may backfire on them. There is a god in heaven. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wasn't there when we needed him. But you never know when he's going to get you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any medical help at all when you had typhoid? Was there any help at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was none. When the English came in, some of them wore masks. And of course, there's a lot of help came in. Social workers and-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the Jewish organizations, HIAS, and the joint-- they would try to pick up whatever is left. And a lot of them, unfortunately, passed away, because they were giving them chocolates and-- and very rich foods.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? The tape is going to end soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I want to stop, and we'll pick up right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Sorry, Amy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, don't--
